title: Bishop Eddie Long, Crusader Against Same-Sex Marriage, Faces Lawsuits Alleging Sexual Coercion of Teenage Boys
teaser: Bishop Eddie Long built a national profile as a defender of traditional marriage and a vocal opponent of same-sex relationships. In September 2010, four young men filed lawsuits alleging he had used his spiritual authority and material gifts to coerce them into sexual relationships that began when they were teenagers. The gap between the pulpit and the allegations was not subtle.
body:
Eddie Long was, by any measure, a significant figure in American evangelical Christianity. As senior pastor of New Birth Missionary Baptist Church in Lithonia, Georgia, he presided over a congregation of roughly 25,000 members, a number that placed his church firmly in the megachurch tier of American religious life. His influence extended well beyond Sunday services, shaping local politics, community organizing, and the national conversation around faith and family values. That influence is precisely what makes the events of September 2010 worth examining with some care.

On September 22, 2010, four young men filed lawsuits against Long, as reported by the New York Times. The lawsuits alleged that Long had used his position as a spiritual authority, combined with gifts and material benefits, to coerce the young men into sexual relationships. According to the reporting, the alleged conduct began when the men were teenagers. The lawsuits did not arrive quietly; they landed in a media environment already primed to notice the distance between the accusations and the man's public record.

That public record was considerable, and specifically relevant. Long had been a leading organizer of a march against same-sex marriage in Atlanta in 2004. The march was not a casual gesture; it was a deliberate, high-profile act of civic and religious advocacy positioning Long as a defender of what he and others called traditional marriage. His activism on the issue was well documented and formed a central part of his national religious identity. He was not merely a pastor who held conventional views; he was someone who organized communities around those views.

The lawsuits alleged a specific mechanism of coercion worth understanding. The young men did not describe a relationship of equals. They described a dynamic in which Long's spiritual authority, his role as a mentor and father figure, and his capacity to provide gifts and opportunities were all used instrumentally to establish and maintain sexual contact. This framing places the allegations squarely within a recognized pattern of institutional religious abuse, where the authority of the office becomes the mechanism of harm. It is a pattern that accountability institutions, legal systems, and journalists have documented repeatedly across denominations.

Long had cultivated a specific kind of relationship with young men in his congregation through a program that brought teenagers and young adults into close mentorship under his guidance. That program was, by all public accounts, presented as a form of spiritual formation and community investment. The plaintiffs alleged it provided something else as well: access. Access is the precondition for the kind of conduct described in the lawsuits, and understanding how it was structured matters to any serious reading of the situation.

The congregation Long led was not a small community gathering in a rented hall. Twenty-five thousand members represents a substantial institutional footprint. At that scale, a pastor is less a spiritual guide and more an institutional leader, with staff, budgets, legal counsel, and communications infrastructure. The gap between that institutional weight and the individuals who filed the lawsuits, who came to Long as teenagers seeking mentorship, is not incidental to the story. It is the story.

It is worth being precise about what was filed and what was not, as of the date of the incident. The lawsuits were civil complaints, not criminal charges. The legal standards differ, and the outcomes in civil litigation against public religious figures have historically varied considerably. The distinction between civil and criminal proceedings is real and legally meaningful. Reporting accurately on allegations requires acknowledging that filing a lawsuit is not equivalent to a court finding of liability. What is not in dispute is that four individuals made serious formal legal claims on a specific date.

Long's public response in the days following the filing was closely watched by religious media and the broader press. His congregation, by virtue of its size and his public prominence, was itself a kind of public institution. How a congregation of 25,000 processes allegations against its founding pastor is itself a matter of public interest, not merely internal church business. The institutional response to credible allegations of misconduct by a senior leader is one of the more reliable indicators of whether accountability is genuine or performative.

The specific combination of elements in this case is worth naming plainly. A pastor who publicly organized opposition to same-sex marriage and relationships stood accused of using his pastoral authority to coerce young men, who had come to him as teenagers, into sexual contact. The contradiction is not simply personal; it is structural. Long did not merely hold private views that conflicted with private behavior. He organized public civic action around those views, lending them the force of community mobilization and moral leadership.

That structural dimension is what distinguishes this incident from ordinary personal failure. A private person who holds views inconsistent with their behavior is a study in human complexity. A public religious leader who uses institutional authority to advocate against a category of relationship, and who then stands accused of using that same institutional authority to engage in alleged conduct with teenagers, has created a specific kind of public accountability problem. Institutional authority, in this reading, is not background; it is the instrument.

For the congregation itself, the September 2010 filings represented an acute crisis. Members who had organized their spiritual and in many cases social lives around Long's leadership faced an immediate and difficult question about the relationship between their faith community and the person who led it. The impact on individual congregants tends to receive less attention than the institutional or celebrity dimensions of such cases, but it is arguably the most consequential human element. Twenty-five thousand people had a stake in what happened next.

Long's story became a significant chapter in the broader ongoing reckoning with clergy misconduct and institutional religious accountability in American life. The pattern of a prominent anti-gay religious figure facing allegations of same-sex conduct was not new in 2010, and the specific addition of alleged coercion of teenagers placed it in an even more serious category of concern. Scholars and journalists who track these patterns note that the public advocacy itself, the marches, the sermons, the political organizing, often functions as a kind of credibility shield that makes the eventual allegations more devastating when they emerge.

What the September 2010 lawsuits established, without adjudication, was a factual predicate for public accountability: four individuals made formal legal claims alleging serious misconduct by a man who had used his public platform to advocate, loudly and organizationally, for a particular vision of sexual morality. That predicate is the appropriate starting point for accountability journalism, which does not require a verdict to identify a meaningful public contradiction. The distance between the 2004 march and the 2010 lawsuits is measurable in years; the distance between the public persona and the allegations is harder to quantify, but it is not small.
subjectTitle: Senior Pastor, New Birth Missionary Baptist Church
